---
title: "Notebooks, the IDE & Colab"
module: "M1 — Python for AI"
chapter: 14
difficulty: "beginner"
prereqs:
  - "M1 Ch1 — Variables & types"
  - "M1 Ch13 — Environments & packages"
learning_objectives:
  - "Name the main places you can write and run Python: an in-browser notebook, a local IDE, Jupyter, and a cloud notebook"
  - "Explain that each place runs the same Python and differs mainly in install, cost, and available compute"
  - "Pick the right place for a task: quick learning, a real project, or work that needs a GPU"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 14
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
review_cards:
  - id: same-python
    q: "How do an in-browser notebook, a local IDE, and Colab relate to each other?"
    a: "They're all places to run the same Python. They differ in install effort, cost, and how much compute (e.g. a GPU) you can reach — not in the language itself."
  - id: which-place
    q: "Which place fits quick learning, which fits a real project, and which fits GPU work?"
    a: "An in-browser notebook for quick learning (zero install, ₹0); a local code editor / IDE for a real multi-file project; a free cloud notebook (Colab or Kaggle) when you need a GPU."
  - id: colab-gpu
    q: "How do you get a free GPU on Google Colab?"
    a: "Open the notebook in Colab, then Runtime → Change runtime type → choose a GPU. Kaggle offers a similar free GPU notebook."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=beginner >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

You've written real Python over thirteen chapters — and every line of it ran right
here, in a notebook inside your browser, with nothing to install. That's one place
to write code. There are a few others, and choosing well saves you hours.

Think of it like a kitchen. A microwave is fast and needs no setup — great for a
quick snack. A full stove is more work to set up but right for a real meal. A
restaurant kitchen has equipment you'd never buy for home. None is "better"; you
pick by the job. Writing Python is the same: the same language runs in a browser
notebook, a code editor on your laptop, or a cloud machine with a GPU. This chapter
is the *what and when* of those places.

> You'll need from before: that you `import` packages and run cells here (Ch1), and
> that each project keeps its packages in an environment you install (Ch13).

---

## The same Python, in different places

Here's the idea to hold onto: **a place to write code is not a different language.**
`for`, `def`, and `import` mean the same thing everywhere. What changes between
places is three practical things:

- **Install** — how much you set up before the first line runs.
- **Cost** — whether it's free (₹0) or costs money.
- **Compute** — how much hardware you can reach, especially a **GPU** (a chip that
  trains models far faster than a laptop's processor).

With that lens, here are the four places you'll meet in this course.

### 1. An in-browser notebook (where you are now)

A **notebook** is a page of **cells** — small blocks you run one at a time — mixed
with prose. The one you're reading runs Python *in the browser itself* through a
runtime called **Pyodide**: zero install, ₹0, nothing to break. It's the best place
to **learn** and to try a quick idea, which is why the whole of M1 lives here.

### 2. A local code editor / IDE

An **IDE** (Integrated Development Environment) is an app on your own machine for
writing code — **VS Code** and **PyCharm** are common ones. Your code lives in
files on your disk, runs against the virtual environment you set up in Ch13, and
the editor adds tools: autocomplete, a debugger, and search across many files. This
is the home of a **real project** — anything with more than one file that you'll
keep and grow.

### 3. Jupyter notebooks

**Jupyter** is the notebook format the browser page imitates, run on your own
machine. Same cells-plus-prose feel, but with your local environment and files
behind it. It shines for **data exploration**: load a table, plot it, tweak a cell,
re-run, repeat — keeping the story and the output side by side.

### 4. A free cloud notebook with a GPU

When a task needs more muscle than a laptop — training a neural network, say —
you open a notebook on a **cloud** machine that has a free GPU. **Google Colab**
and **Kaggle** both give you this at ₹0. You'll use them from Module 5 on; your
laptop never has to do the heavy lifting.

> 💡 **On a free cloud notebook (not here).** This page runs in your browser, so
> there's no GPU to switch on right here — but the workflow elsewhere is short.
> On **Google Colab** you open the notebook in Colab, then go to **Runtime →
> Change runtime type** and choose a **GPU**; the same notebook now runs on a free
> cloud GPU. **Kaggle** is a similar free option: create a notebook, then turn on a
> GPU accelerator in its settings panel. You write the same Python either way — only
> the machine under it changes.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 🧱 For Java developers

A notebook runs **cell by cell**, each cell sharing one live session — closer to a
JShell REPL than to a compiled program: there's no build step, and state from an
earlier cell is still around. Your edit–compile–run loop in IntelliJ maps onto a
**local IDE** here: VS Code or PyCharm is the "real project" home in both worlds,
with a debugger and project-wide search. The rule of thumb carries over — explore
and demo in a notebook, but build and ship anything multi-file in the IDE.
:::

> ⚠️ Watch out: because notebook cells share one session, running them out of order
> can give confusing results (a variable from a cell you ran earlier lingers). When
> in doubt, re-run from the top.

---

## Let's build it (worked example)

You can't fabricate a GPU here, but you *can* ask the running Python where it lives.
The cell below reports the current runtime — proof that "the same Python" is a real,
inspectable thing. Press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "What runtime am I in right now?"
import sys
import platform

print("Python version:", sys.version.split()[0])
print("Platform:", platform.platform())
print("You are running in a browser notebook right now (Pyodide), zero install.")
```

The exact strings depend on the build, but the point stands: this is ordinary
Python, reporting on itself. Open the *same* notebook in Colab with a GPU and the
identical code runs — the platform line would change, your Python would not.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "A tiny helper that names the place for a task"
def place_for(task):
    """Name a sensible place to do `task`."""
    homes = {
        "learn": "in-browser notebook (₹0)",
        "project": "local IDE",
        "gpu": "cloud notebook (Colab/Kaggle)",
    }
    return homes.get(task, "in-browser notebook (₹0)")

for task in ["learn", "project", "gpu"]:
    print(task, "->", place_for(task))
```

Same language, three answers — the choice is about the job, not the syntax.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. An in-browser notebook, a local IDE, Jupyter, and a cloud notebook are all
   **places to run the same Python** — the language doesn't change.
2. They differ in **install, cost, and compute**: the browser is zero-install and
   ₹0; a local IDE owns your files; a cloud notebook reaches a free GPU.
3. **Pick by the job:** a browser notebook to learn or try a quick idea, a **local
   IDE** for a real multi-file project, **Colab or Kaggle** when you need a GPU.
4. Notebook cells share one session, so re-run from the top if results look off.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢

Complete `where_to_run` so it picks a place based on whether the task needs a GPU.
Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_where
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_where
def where_to_run(needs_gpu):
    """Return where to run: Colab for GPU work, else the in-browser notebook."""
    if needs_gpu:
        return "Colab (free GPU)"
    return ____  # TODO: the zero-install, ₹0 place for quick work

run_tests([
    ("gpu work goes to Colab", where_to_run(True), "Colab (free GPU)"),
    ("no gpu stays in browser", where_to_run(False), "in-browser (₹0)"),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

When no GPU is needed, the answer is the place you're in now — the in-browser
notebook, which is free. The expected text is exactly `"in-browser (₹0)"`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def where_to_run(needs_gpu):
    if needs_gpu:
        return "Colab (free GPU)"
    return "in-browser (₹0)"

run_tests([
    ("gpu work goes to Colab", where_to_run(True), "Colab (free GPU)"),
    ("no gpu stays in browser", where_to_run(False), "in-browser (₹0)"),
])
```

When `needs_gpu` is `True` the function returns early with the Colab answer;
otherwise it falls through to the in-browser, ₹0 option.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡

Write `recommend(needs_gpu, real_project)` using `if`/`elif`/`else`: GPU work wins
first, then a real project, otherwise the in-browser notebook. Complete the marked
lines.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_recommend
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_recommend
def recommend(needs_gpu, real_project):
    """Recommend a place: GPU work first, then a real project, else learning."""
    if needs_gpu:
        return "Colab or Kaggle"
    elif real_project:
        return ____   # TODO: the home of a real multi-file project
    else:
        return ____   # TODO: the place for quick learning

run_tests([
    ("gpu wins", recommend(True, True), "Colab or Kaggle"),
    ("project -> IDE", recommend(False, True), "local IDE"),
    ("otherwise notebook", recommend(False, False), "in-browser notebook"),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

The `elif` branch handles a real project — that's the `"local IDE"`. The final
`else` is the everyday case, `"in-browser notebook"`. Note `needs_gpu` is checked
first, so a GPU task never reaches the other branches.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def recommend(needs_gpu, real_project):
    if needs_gpu:
        return "Colab or Kaggle"
    elif real_project:
        return "local IDE"
    else:
        return "in-browser notebook"

run_tests([
    ("gpu wins", recommend(True, True), "Colab or Kaggle"),
    ("project -> IDE", recommend(False, True), "local IDE"),
    ("otherwise notebook", recommend(False, False), "in-browser notebook"),
])
```

The branches are checked top to bottom: `needs_gpu` first, so GPU work always
routes to Colab or Kaggle; then a real project goes to the local IDE; everything
else stays in the in-browser notebook.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). In two or three sentences,
describe where *you* will write code for the rest of this course and why. Name at
least two of the four places and the kind of task each is for.

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Names at least **two** of the four places (in-browser notebook, local IDE,
  Jupyter, cloud notebook).
- Ties each named place to a **task** (quick learning, a real project, or GPU work).
- Mentions that the choice is about install/cost/compute, not a different language.

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
plan = {
    "learning M1–M4": "in-browser notebook (₹0, nothing to install)",
    "my own project": "local IDE (VS Code, files on my machine)",
    "training a model in M5+": "Colab or Kaggle (free GPU)",
}
for task, place in plan.items():
    print(f"{task}: {place}")
```

"I'll keep learning here in the browser because it's ₹0 and there's nothing to set
up. When I start my own multi-file project I'll move to VS Code so I get a debugger
and my files on disk. Once I need to train a model in Module 5 I'll open a Colab or
Kaggle notebook for the free GPU — same Python throughout, with different machines
under it."
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch14.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You now know the four places you'll write Python — and that picking one is about the
job (quick learning, a real project, or needing a GPU), never about a different
language. That closes the M1 lesson chapters. Next come the **module quiz**, which
checks the whole of your Python foundation, and the **data-cleaning capstone**, where
you turn the messy `habits.csv` into a tidy table with the functions you've built.
